**spleen-anatomy-overview.md** (Spleen anatomy overview)

# Spleen — Anatomy Overview

The spleen is a single, unpaired organ located in the upper left portion of
the abdomen, tucked beneath the left side of the rib cage, posterior and
lateral to the stomach and superior to the left kidney. It is the smallest of
the four organs typically segmented in an abdominal MRI study covering the
liver, kidneys, and spleen, and normally shows a correspondingly smaller
per-slice coverage percentage than the liver.

Functionally, the spleen filters blood, recycles old red blood cells, stores
platelets, and participates in immune responses. It has a relatively
homogeneous internal texture on imaging, similar in some respects to liver
parenchyma but generally smaller and more variable in exact size and shape
from person to person and with respiration phase. Because it is smaller and
sits near the edge of a typical axial slice, it is more prone to partial
volume effects — where a slice cuts through only part of the organ — which
can affect both manual and automated measurements more than for larger organs
like the liver.

---
**abdominal-cavity-overview.md** (How the liver, kidneys, and spleen sit relative to each other)

# Abdominal Cavity — Relative Organ Layout

On a single axial (cross-sectional) abdominal MRI slice, the four organs
segmented in this project occupy characteristic, roughly consistent relative
positions, though the exact slice level determines which organs are visible
and how much of each appears:

- The **liver** occupies the upper right and central abdomen, and is the
  largest organ by cross-sectional area on most slices where it appears.
- The **right kidney** sits in the right retroperitoneum, typically slightly
  lower than the left kidney because the liver displaces it downward.
- The **left kidney** sits in the left retroperitoneum, at a similar or
  slightly higher level than the right kidney.
- The **spleen** sits in the upper left abdomen, roughly at a similar
  craniocaudal level to the upper liver and left kidney, but with a smaller
  cross-sectional footprint.

Because these organs sit at different craniocaudal levels, not every organ
appears on every slice — a slice near the top of the liver may not yet show
the kidneys, and a slice at the level of the mid-kidneys may show a much
smaller sliver of liver or none of the spleen. This is a normal consequence of
3D anatomy being sampled by a single 2D slice, not a segmentation error.

---
**spleen-normal-size-mri-t2.md** (Normal spleen size and signal reference range on T2 MRI)

# Spleen — Normal Size and Appearance on T2-Weighted MRI

Normal spleen length is commonly cited as up to roughly 12-13 cm along its
long axis in an average adult, though normal size varies with patient height,
body habitus, and measurement technique. Splenomegaly (an enlarged spleen) is
a recognized clinical finding, but — as with the other organs in this
project — a single 2D axial slice's coverage percentage is only a partial,
local indicator and cannot by itself confirm or exclude splenomegaly, which is
properly assessed from the full 3D volume or a dedicated long-axis measurement.

On T2-weighted sequences, normal splenic parenchyma is relatively homogeneous
and, like the liver and kidneys, benefits from fat suppression (T2-SPIR) for
improved contrast against adjacent retroperitoneal and perisplenic fat. Because
the spleen is the smallest of the four segmented organs, small absolute
measurement errors translate into larger relative (percentage) differences
than they would for the liver, which is a useful context note when interpreting
per-organ confidence or coverage statistics for the spleen specifically.

---
**mri-slice-plane-and-coverage-basics.md** (Why single-slice coverage percentage is not the same as organ volume)

# Single-Slice Coverage vs. Whole-Organ Volume

This project analyzes individual 2D axial MRI slices, and reports each
organ's coverage as a percentage of that single slice's pixels. This is a
deliberately local measurement and should not be interpreted as a statement
about the organ's total volume, its size relative to "normal" whole-organ
reference ranges, or its size in 3D.

A few concrete consequences of this slice-based approach:

- The same organ will show very different coverage percentages at different
  slice levels — a mid-liver slice shows far more liver than a slice near the
  liver's superior or inferior edge.
- An organ can be completely or partially absent from a given slice simply
  because that slice does not intersect the organ at all, not because the
  organ is abnormal or the segmentation failed.
- Comparing one organ's coverage percentage to another organ's coverage
  percentage on the *same* slice is only meaningful as a rough relative
  indicator (e.g., "the liver takes up much more of this slice than the
  spleen does"), not as a precise volumetric comparison.

Any clinical-sounding statement about whether an organ is "enlarged" or
"reduced" in size should be understood as relating only to this single slice,
and any full assessment of organ size would require a 3D volumetric analysis
across the entire scan, which is outside the scope of a per-slice report.

**kidneys-normal-size-mri-t2.md** (Normal kidney size and expected left/right symmetry on T2 MRI)

# Kidneys — Normal Size and Symmetry on T2-Weighted MRI

Adult kidney length is commonly cited in the range of roughly 9-13 cm,
measured along the long axis in coronal or sagittal planes; width and
thickness are each smaller than length, giving the kidney its characteristic
bean shape. As with any single-organ measurement, a single axial slice only
captures a cross-section at that level, not the full long-axis length, so
per-slice area/coverage should be interpreted as a local snapshot rather than
a whole-organ size measurement.

The kidneys are one of the few paired organs where a meaningful degree of
left-right size difference is still considered normal — differences up to
roughly 1-1.5 cm in length, or a modest percentage difference in per-slice
area, are commonly within normal anatomical variation, partly because the
right kidney is displaced slightly downward by the liver. Substantially larger
asymmetries, or a unilateral difference paired with a shape or signal
abnormality, are more suggestive of a true finding requiring clinical review.

On T2-weighted imaging, normal renal parenchyma shows a corticomedullary
signal pattern (cortex versus medulla contrast), and fat suppression (T2-SPIR)
improves the contrast between kidney tissue and surrounding perinephric fat.

---

# Kidneys — Anatomy Overview

The kidneys are a pair of bean-shaped organs located in the retroperitoneum,
one on each side of the spine, roughly at the level of the lower ribs and
upper lumbar vertebrae. Each kidney filters blood — together processing on
the order of half a cup of blood per minute — removing waste products and
excess fluid, which are excreted as urine, while helping regulate fluid
balance, electrolytes, and blood pressure via hormone production.

Internally, each kidney contains roughly a million nephrons, each with a
glomerulus (the filtering unit) and a system of tubules that reclaim needed
water and solutes before urine passes to the ureter. Because the right kidney
sits just below the liver, it is typically positioned slightly lower than the
left kidney in most people — a normal anatomical asymmetry, not a pathological
finding. On cross-sectional imaging, the kidneys appear as two separate,
roughly symmetric organs; substantial size or signal differences between the
two beyond normal variation can be a reason for closer review.

---

**liver-normal-size-mri-t2.md** (Normal liver size and signal reference range on T2 MRI)

# Liver — Normal Size and Appearance on T2-Weighted MRI

Craniocaudal (top-to-bottom) liver span is commonly cited as roughly 10-15 cm
along the mid-clavicular line in an average adult, though this varies with
patient size, body habitus, and respiratory phase at the time of scanning.
Because craniocaudal span is a 3D measurement, it cannot be read directly off
a single 2D axial slice — an individual slice's organ area only reflects
coverage at that slice level, not the organ's total length or volume.

On T2-weighted sequences, healthy liver parenchyma has intermediate-to-low
signal intensity relative to fluid-filled structures (which appear bright).
With fat suppression (as in T2-SPIR), the background fat signal is reduced,
which improves contrast between the liver and surrounding fat and makes organ
boundaries easier to delineate for both manual reading and automated
segmentation. A liver that is markedly enlarged (hepatomegaly) or shows an
irregular contour on imaging warrants clinical correlation; single-slice
segmentation coverage alone cannot establish or rule out hepatomegaly.

---
**liver-anatomy-overview.md** (Liver anatomy overview)

# Liver — Anatomy Overview

The liver is the largest solid organ in the abdomen, situated in the upper right
portion of the abdominal cavity, mostly tucked under the right side of the rib
cage and diaphragm. It spans across the midline, with a large right lobe and a
smaller left lobe, separated internally by connective tissue and blood vessel
boundaries rather than a visible surface groove in most imaging planes.

Functionally, the liver filters blood arriving from the digestive tract,
metabolizes nutrients and drugs, produces bile for fat digestion, synthesizes
plasma proteins, and stores glycogen and certain vitamins. Because of this
central metabolic role, it receives a dual blood supply: the hepatic artery
(oxygen-rich blood) and the portal vein (nutrient-rich blood from the gut).

On cross-sectional imaging, the liver is a single unpaired organ occupying a
large fraction of the upper abdomen, so it typically shows the largest
coverage percentage of any organ segmented on a single axial abdominal slice.
Its texture is normally homogeneous; focal variation or unexpected contour
changes are more likely to reflect pathology, imaging artifact, or slice
positioning than true anatomical variation.

right kidney is displaced slightly downward by the liver. Substantially larger
asymmetries, or a unilateral difference paired with a shape or signal
abnormality, are more suggestive of a true finding requiring clinical review.

signal pattern (cortex versus medulla contrast), and fat suppression (T2-SPIR)
improves the contrast between kidney tissue and surrounding perinephric fat.

**kidneys-anatomy-overview.md** (Paired kidney anatomy and function)

# Kidneys — Anatomy Overview

The kidneys are a pair of bean-shaped organs located in the retroperitoneum,
one on each side of the spine, roughly at the level of the lower ribs and
upper lumbar vertebrae. Each kidney filters blood — together processing on
the order of half a cup of blood per minute — removing waste products and
excess fluid, which are excreted as urine, while helping regulate fluid
balance, electrolytes, and blood pressure via hormone production.

Internally, each kidney contains roughly a million nephrons, each with a
glomerulus (the filtering unit) and a system of tubules that reclaim needed
water and solutes before urine passes to the ureter. Because the right kidney
sits just below the liver, it is typically positioned slightly lower than the
left kidney in most people — a normal anatomical asymmetry, not a pathological
finding. On cross-sectional imaging, the kidneys appear as two separate,
roughly symmetric organs; substantial size or signal differences between the
two beyond normal variation can be a reason for closer review.

---

# Abdominal Cavity — Relative Organ Layout

On a single axial (cross-sectional) abdominal MRI slice, the four organs
segmented in this project occupy characteristic, roughly consistent relative
positions, though the exact slice level determines which organs are visible
and how much of each appears:

- The **liver** occupies the upper right and central abdomen, and is the
  largest organ by cross-sectional area on most slices where it appears.
- The **right kidney** sits in the right retroperitoneum, typically slightly
  lower than the left kidney because the liver displaces it downward.
- The **left kidney** sits in the left retroperitoneum, at a similar or
  slightly higher level than the right kidney.
- The **spleen** sits in the upper left abdomen, roughly at a similar
  craniocaudal level to the upper liver and left kidney, but with a smaller
  cross-sectional footprint.

Because these organs sit at different craniocaudal levels, not every organ
appears on every slice — a slice near the top of the liver may not yet show
the kidneys, and a slice at the level of the mid-kidneys may show a much
smaller sliver of liver or none of the spleen. This is a normal consequence of
3D anatomy being sampled by a single 2D slice, not a segmentation error.

# Spleen — Normal Size and Appearance on T2-Weighted MRI

Normal spleen length is commonly cited as up to roughly 12-13 cm along its
long axis in an average adult, though normal size varies with patient height,
body habitus, and measurement technique. Splenomegaly (an enlarged spleen) is
a recognized clinical finding, but — as with the other organs in this
project — a single 2D axial slice's coverage percentage is only a partial,
local indicator and cannot by itself confirm or exclude splenomegaly, which is
properly assessed from the full 3D volume or a dedicated long-axis measurement.

On T2-weighted sequences, normal splenic parenchyma is relatively homogeneous
and, like the liver and kidneys, benefits from fat suppression (T2-SPIR) for
improved contrast against adjacent retroperitoneal and perisplenic fat. Because
the spleen is the smallest of the four segmented organs, small absolute
measurement errors translate into larger relative (percentage) differences
than they would for the liver, which is a useful context note when interpreting
per-organ confidence or coverage statistics for the spleen specifically.

**organ-asymmetry-paired-structures.md** (Normal left/right kidney asymmetry ranges)

# Paired Organ Asymmetry — Kidneys

Of the four organs in this project, only the kidneys are a paired, bilaterally
symmetric structure (the liver and spleen are single, unpaired organs, so
left/right comparison does not apply to them). Because kidneys are paired,
some degree of left-right difference in per-slice coverage or measured size is
expected and normal, not necessarily a sign of a problem.

Contributing factors to normal kidney asymmetry include:

- The right kidney is typically displaced slightly downward relative to the
  left kidney due to the liver occupying space above it, so at a given axial
  slice level the two kidneys may show different amounts of tissue simply due
  to this vertical offset.
- Modest individual anatomical variation in kidney size exists between
  people, and can also exist between a person's own two kidneys, generally
  within a small percentage of each other.

A rough guideline used informally in this project: a left/right coverage
difference under roughly 30% of the larger value is treated as within
plausible normal variation, while a larger difference is flagged as
noteworthy and worth mentioning for human review, since it could reflect a
true asymmetry, a slice-level artifact, or a segmentation error, all of which
are better resolved by a human reader than assumed away.

---
**kidneys-anatomy-overview.md** (Paired kidney anatomy and function)

# Kidneys — Anatomy Overview

The kidneys are a pair of bean-shaped organs located in the retroperitoneum,
one on each side of the spine, roughly at the level of the lower ribs and
upper lumbar vertebrae. Each kidney filters blood — together processing on
the order of half a cup of blood per minute — removing waste products and
excess fluid, which are excreted as urine, while helping regulate fluid
balance, electrolytes, and blood pressure via hormone production.

Internally, each kidney contains roughly a million nephrons, each with a
glomerulus (the filtering unit) and a system of tubules that reclaim needed
water and solutes before urine passes to the ureter. Because the right kidney
sits just below the liver, it is typically positioned slightly lower than the
left kidney in most people — a normal anatomical asymmetry, not a pathological
finding. On cross-sectional imaging, the kidneys appear as two separate,
roughly symmetric organs; substantial size or signal differences between the
two beyond normal variation can be a reason for closer review.

---
**kidneys-normal-size-mri-t2.md** (Normal kidney size and expected left/right symmetry on T2 MRI)

# Kidneys — Normal Size and Symmetry on T2-Weighted MRI

Adult kidney length is commonly cited in the range of roughly 9-13 cm,
measured along the long axis in coronal or sagittal planes; width and
thickness are each smaller than length, giving the kidney its characteristic
bean shape. As with any single-organ measurement, a single axial slice only
captures a cross-section at that level, not the full long-axis length, so
per-slice area/coverage should be interpreted as a local snapshot rather than
a whole-organ size measurement.

The kidneys are one of the few paired organs where a meaningful degree of
left-right size difference is still considered normal — differences up to
roughly 1-1.5 cm in length, or a modest percentage difference in per-slice
area, are commonly within normal anatomical variation, partly because the
right kidney is displaced slightly downward by the liver. Substantially larger
asymmetries, or a unilateral difference paired with a shape or signal
abnormality, are more suggestive of a true finding requiring clinical review.

On T2-weighted imaging, normal renal parenchyma shows a corticomedullary
signal pattern (cortex versus medulla contrast), and fat suppression (T2-SPIR)
improves the contrast between kidney tissue and surrounding perinephric fat.

coverage percentage of any organ segmented on a single axial abdominal slice.

positioning than true anatomical variation.

segmented in this project occupy characteristic, roughly consistent relative
positions, though the exact slice level determines which organs are visible
and how much of each appears:

- The **liver** occupies the upper right and central abdomen, and is the
  largest organ by cross-sectional area on most slices where it appears.
- The **right kidney** sits in the right retroperitoneum, typically slightly
  lower than the left kidney because the liver displaces it downward.
- The **left kidney** sits in the left retroperitoneum, at a similar or
  slightly higher level than the right kidney.
- The **spleen** sits in the upper left abdomen, roughly at a similar
  craniocaudal level to the upper liver and left kidney, but with a smaller
  cross-sectional footprint.

Because these organs sit at different craniocaudal levels, not every organ
appears on every slice — a slice near the top of the liver may not yet show
the kidneys, and a slice at the level of the mid-kidneys may show a much
smaller sliver of liver or none of the spleen. This is a normal consequence of
3D anatomy being sampled by a single 2D slice, not a segmentation error.

---
**spleen-normal-size-mri-t2.md** (Normal spleen size and signal reference range on T2 MRI)

# Spleen — Normal Size and Appearance on T2-Weighted MRI

Normal spleen length is commonly cited as up to roughly 12-13 cm along its
long axis in an average adult, though normal size varies with patient height,
body habitus, and measurement technique. Splenomegaly (an enlarged spleen) is
a recognized clinical finding, but — as with the other organs in this
project — a single 2D axial slice's coverage percentage is only a partial,
local indicator and cannot by itself confirm or exclude splenomegaly, which is
properly assessed from the full 3D volume or a dedicated long-axis measurement.

On T2-weighted sequences, normal splenic parenchyma is relatively homogeneous
and, like the liver and kidneys, benefits from fat suppression (T2-SPIR) for
improved contrast against adjacent retroperitoneal and perisplenic fat. Because
the spleen is the smallest of the four segmented organs, small absolute
measurement errors translate into larger relative (percentage) differences
than they would for the liver, which is a useful context note when interpreting
per-organ confidence or coverage statistics for the spleen specifically.

---
**mri-slice-plane-and-coverage-basics.md** (Why single-slice coverage percentage is not the same as organ volume)

# Single-Slice Coverage vs. Whole-Organ Volume

This project analyzes individual 2D axial MRI slices, and reports each
organ's coverage as a percentage of that single slice's pixels. This is a
deliberately local measurement and should not be interpreted as a statement
about the organ's total volume, its size relative to "normal" whole-organ
reference ranges, or its size in 3D.

A few concrete consequences of this slice-based approach:

- The same organ will show very different coverage percentages at different
  slice levels — a mid-liver slice shows far more liver than a slice near the
  liver's superior or inferior edge.
- An organ can be completely or partially absent from a given slice simply
  because that slice does not intersect the organ at all, not because the
  organ is abnormal or the segmentation failed.
- Comparing one organ's coverage percentage to another organ's coverage
  percentage on the *same* slice is only meaningful as a rough relative
  indicator (e.g., "the liver takes up much more of this slice than the
  spleen does"), not as a precise volumetric comparison.

Any clinical-sounding statement about whether an organ is "enlarged" or
"reduced" in size should be understood as relating only to this single slice,
and any full assessment of organ size would require a 3D volumetric analysis
across the entire scan, which is outside the scope of a per-slice report.

# Spleen — Anatomy Overview

The spleen is a single, unpaired organ located in the upper left portion of
the abdomen, tucked beneath the left side of the rib cage, posterior and
lateral to the stomach and superior to the left kidney. It is the smallest of
the four organs typically segmented in an abdominal MRI study covering the

per-slice coverage percentage than the liver.

platelets, and participates in immune responses. It has a relatively
homogeneous internal texture on imaging, similar in some respects to liver
parenchyma but generally smaller and more variable in exact size and shape
from person to person and with respiration phase. Because it is smaller and
sits near the edge of a typical axial slice, it is more prone to partial
volume effects — where a slice cuts through only part of the organ — which
can affect both manual and automated measurements more than for larger organs
like the liver.

---
**kidneys-normal-size-mri-t2.md** (Normal kidney size and expected left/right symmetry on T2 MRI)

# Kidneys — Normal Size and Symmetry on T2-Weighted MRI

Adult kidney length is commonly cited in the range of roughly 9-13 cm,
measured along the long axis in coronal or sagittal planes; width and
thickness are each smaller than length, giving the kidney its characteristic
bean shape. As with any single-organ measurement, a single axial slice only
captures a cross-section at that level, not the full long-axis length, so
per-slice area/coverage should be interpreted as a local snapshot rather than
a whole-organ size measurement.

The kidneys are one of the few paired organs where a meaningful degree of
left-right size difference is still considered normal — differences up to
roughly 1-1.5 cm in length, or a modest percentage difference in per-slice
area, are commonly within normal anatomical variation, partly because the
right kidney is displaced slightly downward by the liver. Substantially larger
asymmetries, or a unilateral difference paired with a shape or signal
abnormality, are more suggestive of a true finding requiring clinical review.

On T2-weighted imaging, normal renal parenchyma shows a corticomedullary
signal pattern (cortex versus medulla contrast), and fat suppression (T2-SPIR)
improves the contrast between kidney tissue and surrounding perinephric fat.

# Kidneys — Anatomy Overview

The kidneys are a pair of bean-shaped organs located in the retroperitoneum,
one on each side of the spine, roughly at the level of the lower ribs and
upper lumbar vertebrae. Each kidney filters blood — together processing on
the order of half a cup of blood per minute — removing waste products and
excess fluid, which are excreted as urine, while helping regulate fluid
balance, electrolytes, and blood pressure via hormone production.

Internally, each kidney contains roughly a million nephrons, each with a
glomerulus (the filtering unit) and a system of tubules that reclaim needed
water and solutes before urine passes to the ureter. Because the right kidney
sits just below the liver, it is typically positioned slightly lower than the
left kidney in most people — a normal anatomical asymmetry, not a pathological
finding. On cross-sectional imaging, the kidneys appear as two separate,
roughly symmetric organs; substantial size or signal differences between the
two beyond normal variation can be a reason for closer review.

---

**mri-slice-plane-and-coverage-basics.md** (Why single-slice coverage percentage is not the same as organ volume)

# Single-Slice Coverage vs. Whole-Organ Volume

This project analyzes individual 2D axial MRI slices, and reports each
organ's coverage as a percentage of that single slice's pixels. This is a
deliberately local measurement and should not be interpreted as a statement
about the organ's total volume, its size relative to "normal" whole-organ
reference ranges, or its size in 3D.

  slice levels — a mid-liver slice shows far more liver than a slice near the
  liver's superior or inferior edge.

  indicator (e.g., "the liver takes up much more of this slice than the
  spleen does"), not as a precise volumetric comparison.

"reduced" in size should be understood as relating only to this single slice,
and any full assessment of organ size would require a 3D volumetric analysis
across the entire scan, which is outside the scope of a per-slice report.

# Liver — Normal Size and Appearance on T2-Weighted MRI

Craniocaudal (top-to-bottom) liver span is commonly cited as roughly 10-15 cm
along the mid-clavicular line in an average adult, though this varies with
patient size, body habitus, and respiratory phase at the time of scanning.

boundaries easier to delineate for both manual reading and automated
segmentation. A liver that is markedly enlarged (hepatomegaly) or shows an
irregular contour on imaging warrants clinical correlation; single-slice
segmentation coverage alone cannot establish or rule out hepatomegaly.

# Liver — Anatomy Overview

The liver is the largest solid organ in the abdomen, situated in the upper right
portion of the abdominal cavity, mostly tucked under the right side of the rib
cage and diaphragm. It spans across the midline, with a large right lobe and a
smaller left lobe, separated internally by connective tissue and blood vessel
boundaries rather than a visible surface groove in most imaging planes.

Functionally, the liver filters blood arriving from the digestive tract,
metabolizes nutrients and drugs, produces bile for fat digestion, synthesizes
plasma proteins, and stores glycogen and certain vitamins. Because of this
central metabolic role, it receives a dual blood supply: the hepatic artery

On cross-sectional imaging, the liver is a single unpaired organ occupying a
large fraction of the upper abdomen, so it typically shows the largest
coverage percentage of any organ segmented on a single axial abdominal slice.
Its texture is normally homogeneous; focal variation or unexpected contour
changes are more likely to reflect pathology, imaging artifact, or slice
positioning than true anatomical variation.

# Kidneys — Anatomy Overview

The kidneys are a pair of bean-shaped organs located in the retroperitoneum,
one on each side of the spine, roughly at the level of the lower ribs and
upper lumbar vertebrae. Each kidney filters blood — together processing on
the order of half a cup of blood per minute — removing waste products and
excess fluid, which are excreted as urine, while helping regulate fluid
balance, electrolytes, and blood pressure via hormone production.

Internally, each kidney contains roughly a million nephrons, each with a
glomerulus (the filtering unit) and a system of tubules that reclaim needed
water and solutes before urine passes to the ureter. Because the right kidney
sits just below the liver, it is typically positioned slightly lower than the
left kidney in most people — a normal anatomical asymmetry, not a pathological
finding. On cross-sectional imaging, the kidneys appear as two separate,
roughly symmetric organs; substantial size or signal differences between the
two beyond normal variation can be a reason for closer review.

**kidneys-anatomy-overview.md** (Paired kidney anatomy and function)

# Kidneys — Anatomy Overview

The kidneys are a pair of bean-shaped organs located in the retroperitoneum,
one on each side of the spine, roughly at the level of the lower ribs and
upper lumbar vertebrae. Each kidney filters blood — together processing on
the order of half a cup of blood per minute — removing waste products and
excess fluid, which are excreted as urine, while helping regulate fluid
balance, electrolytes, and blood pressure via hormone production.

Internally, each kidney contains roughly a million nephrons, each with a
glomerulus (the filtering unit) and a system of tubules that reclaim needed
water and solutes before urine passes to the ureter. Because the right kidney
sits just below the liver, it is typically positioned slightly lower than the
left kidney in most people — a normal anatomical asymmetry, not a pathological
finding. On cross-sectional imaging, the kidneys appear as two separate,
roughly symmetric organs; substantial size or signal differences between the
two beyond normal variation can be a reason for closer review.

---
**kidneys-normal-size-mri-t2.md** (Normal kidney size and expected left/right symmetry on T2 MRI)

# Kidneys — Normal Size and Symmetry on T2-Weighted MRI

Adult kidney length is commonly cited in the range of roughly 9-13 cm,
measured along the long axis in coronal or sagittal planes; width and
thickness are each smaller than length, giving the kidney its characteristic
bean shape. As with any single-organ measurement, a single axial slice only
captures a cross-section at that level, not the full long-axis length, so
per-slice area/coverage should be interpreted as a local snapshot rather than
a whole-organ size measurement.

The kidneys are one of the few paired organs where a meaningful degree of
left-right size difference is still considered normal — differences up to
roughly 1-1.5 cm in length, or a modest percentage difference in per-slice
area, are commonly within normal anatomical variation, partly because the
right kidney is displaced slightly downward by the liver. Substantially larger
asymmetries, or a unilateral difference paired with a shape or signal
abnormality, are more suggestive of a true finding requiring clinical review.

On T2-weighted imaging, normal renal parenchyma shows a corticomedullary
signal pattern (cortex versus medulla contrast), and fat suppression (T2-SPIR)
improves the contrast between kidney tissue and surrounding perinephric fat.

A few concrete consequences of this slice-based approach:

# Spleen — Normal Size and Appearance on T2-Weighted MRI

Normal spleen length is commonly cited as up to roughly 12-13 cm along its
long axis in an average adult, though normal size varies with patient height,
body habitus, and measurement technique. Splenomegaly (an enlarged spleen) is
a recognized clinical finding, but — as with the other organs in this
project — a single 2D axial slice's coverage percentage is only a partial,
local indicator and cannot by itself confirm or exclude splenomegaly, which is
properly assessed from the full 3D volume or a dedicated long-axis measurement.

On T2-weighted sequences, normal splenic parenchyma is relatively homogeneous
and, like the liver and kidneys, benefits from fat suppression (T2-SPIR) for
improved contrast against adjacent retroperitoneal and perisplenic fat. Because
the spleen is the smallest of the four segmented organs, small absolute
measurement errors translate into larger relative (percentage) differences
than they would for the liver, which is a useful context note when interpreting
per-organ confidence or coverage statistics for the spleen specifically.

**kidneys-normal-size-mri-t2.md** (Normal kidney size and expected left/right symmetry on T2 MRI)

# Kidneys — Normal Size and Symmetry on T2-Weighted MRI

Adult kidney length is commonly cited in the range of roughly 9-13 cm,
measured along the long axis in coronal or sagittal planes; width and
thickness are each smaller than length, giving the kidney its characteristic
bean shape. As with any single-organ measurement, a single axial slice only
captures a cross-section at that level, not the full long-axis length, so
per-slice area/coverage should be interpreted as a local snapshot rather than
a whole-organ size measurement.

The kidneys are one of the few paired organs where a meaningful degree of
left-right size difference is still considered normal — differences up to
roughly 1-1.5 cm in length, or a modest percentage difference in per-slice
area, are commonly within normal anatomical variation, partly because the
right kidney is displaced slightly downward by the liver. Substantially larger
asymmetries, or a unilateral difference paired with a shape or signal
abnormality, are more suggestive of a true finding requiring clinical review.

On T2-weighted imaging, normal renal parenchyma shows a corticomedullary
signal pattern (cortex versus medulla contrast), and fat suppression (T2-SPIR)
improves the contrast between kidney tissue and surrounding perinephric fat.
